do hereby make oath and state the following: 
1. 1am a trustee of the Penvaan Property Trust (“the Trust”), duly authorised by 
resolution of the trustees of the Trust who have unanimously authorised me to 
oppose this application and to respond to the application for leave to appeal to 
the Constitutional Court in terms of Constitutional Court Rule 19(4)(a).
Page |f2 
The resolution is annexed hereto, marked “A”, to avoid any dispute pertaining 
thereto. 
In order for this Honourable Court to consider and determine this application, and 
to view it in proper perspective, it is necessary to annex hereto a copy of the 
Trust Deed, marked “B”, which has unfortunately been omitted, for no apparent 
reason. 
This document, although referred to in the judgment must be interpreted in 
context to consider the fact that this application has no merit. To complete the 
relevant and necessary documents, | annex hereto a letter by Mr Volker an 
erstwhile trustee, marked “C”. 
The application papers were served on the Respondents’ attorneys on 19 June 
2023, without it having been issued by this Court but apparently lodged on 
20 June 2020. If condonation is necessary by the Applicants, the Respondents 
would seek leave to address this issue. 
INTRODUCTION 
4.1 The jurisdiction of the Constitutional Court was widened by the amendment 
to the Constitution effected by the Constitution’s Seventeenth Amendment 
Act.! 
Section 167(3) of the Constitution provides that the Court may decide: 
4.1.1. Constitutional matters; and 
4.1.2. Any other matter which raises an arguable point of law of general 
public importance that this Court ought to consider. 
4 Act 72 of 2012 with effect from 23 August 2013 i
Pagels 
It was also held that the matter must be in the interest of justice for the Court 
to hear the matter.2 
4.2 In summary, the Respondents’ main contentions are: 
4.2.1. That there is no constitutional matter involved; 
4.2.2 That there is no matter which raises an arguable point of law of 
general public importance that this Court should consider. 
It is therefore required that the point must be one of law and it must be arguable 
and it must be of public importance. Arguable in context means that a point of 
law must have some prospects of success.° 
It is contended that there are no prospects of success of the Applicant's 
application for leave to appeal and it would therefore not be in the interest of 
justice for the Court to hear the matter. 
The Applicant seeks leave to appeal on the basis that the issue sought to be 
determined, namely the interpretation of a Trust Deed, raises arguable points of 
law of general public importance that ought to be considered by this Court, which 
has some prospects of success in terms of Section 167(3)(b)(ii) of the 
Constitution. 
It is not contended by the Applicant that the application raises any constitutional 
matter. 
To enable this Court to be clothed with jurisdiction, the Applicant must satisfy the 
test for leave to appeal. 
wa
n Paulsen v Slip Knot Investments 777 (Pty) Ltd 2015 (3) SA 479 (CC) at paras [12] - [31] 
Paulsen supra at para 22 ,

ARGUABLE POINT OF LAW

It is contended that no point of law has been demonstrated by the Applicant. The 
majority of the contentions pertain to an attack on the manner the terms of the 
trust deed has been interpreted. 
The issue which is at the heart of the application is whether, by interpretating the 
Trust Deed of the Penvaan Property Trust, the Trust Deed authorises two of the 
three trustees present at a trustees meeting, in the absence of the third trustee, 
to validly authorise the signing of a deed of suretyship. 
The Respondents contend that the argument advanced by the Applicant has no 
substance and/or merit and constitutes a last attempt, to hold the Trust, liable in 
terms of a suretyship signed by only two of the three trustees and is a disguised 
attempt for the reconsideration of an appeal which was decided on the 
interpretation of the terms of a specific Trust Deed. 
It is contended that the matter decided by the SCA, in simple terms, revolved 
around the interpretation of the terms of a specific Trust Deed and it had no 
regard to terms of other Trust Deeds. 
No reference was made by the Applicant in the papers before the court a quo or 
the SCA of similar terms in other Trust Deeds and the effect thereof pertaining to 
other trust deeds and resolutions passed by Trusts that would bind a Trust, to 
compare it with the provisions of the present Trust Deed. The entire matter at all 
times centred around the interpretation of the Penvaan Property trust deed. 
It is contended that the terms of all Trusts are not on all fours with each other or 
the present Trust Deed, whether inter vivos or testamentary trusts and that each 
trust deed must be interpreted in accordance with its own peculiar provisions. 
The mere reading of the authorities referred to in the judgments in the SCA 
demonstrates clearly that the terms of all trust deeds are not similar. It is not

necessary to expand thereon by virtue of the fact that the relevant terms are 
referred to in the judgment of the SCA and which would be eluded to hereunder. 
it must be accepted that each trust would be modelled by the founder to 
accommodate the needs and purpose of the trust, it is created for. A trust deed 
is not subject to any legislative prescripts that prescribe the terms that a trust 
deed must contain, save that the Trust Property Control Act No 57 of 1988 
prescribe for certain formalities to be complied with. 
There is no evidence before this Court that there is a standard format of a trust 
deed that must be ascribed to by any donors. 
The SCA has expressed itself on the interpretation of the trust deed by the 
majority, despite two Justices concurring with the majority but arriving at the 
same decision on the matter on a limited basis only. 
There is therefore no dissenting or minority judgment. The judgment of the SCA 
confirmed the judgment of the court a quo by Bezuidenhout AJ (as she then was). 
If the judgment of the SCA, is carefully scrutinised, it cannot be held that it 
consists of a majority decision and minority decision, as alluded to by the 
Applicant.* All 5 Judges reached the same conclusion, the one judgment was 
based on the appendix and the main terms of the Trust Deed and the other 
judgment only on the terms embodied in the appendix. 
The judgment of the SCA does not raise any new and difficult issues. It did not 
decide any question of law at all. The SCA in fact interpretated only one specific 
Trust Deed, which is not generic in its content and/or clauses and found 
confirmation of their view in other decided case of the SCA and other divisions. 
This application is an attempt to have the appeal re-argued in a disguised form. 
If regard is had to the application, the majority of the contents relate to an 
argument that the Court a quo was wrong in its findings based on interpretation. 
Para 34 of the judgment: Supreme Court of Appeal
Page |6 
The Applicant in this application does not request any legal principle to be 
revisited or that the test for the interpretation of documents, as applied by the 
SCA, be revisited. 
BACKGROUND FACTS 
22. Before responding to the factual, peripheral issues referred to in the Applicant's 
statement, it would be necessary to record certain factual issues which are not 
in dispute and then to deal with the interpretation of the trust deed. The factual 
issues are inter alia the following: 
22.1 The deponent to the statement Mrs De Wet was the erstwhile attorney of 
Mrs Volker who was a trustee of the Trust prior to her sequestration; 
22.2 The Applicant represented Mrs Volker in her divorce action against her 
husband; 
22.3 Mrs De Wet drafted the agenda of a meeting where the resolution to agree 
to the signing of a suretyship would be tabled; 
22.4 Mrs De Wet was the author of the suretyship document; 
22.5 Mrs De Wet was in possession of the Trust Deed when the suretyship 
document was drafted by it; 
22.6 Mrs De Wet was the instructing attorney representing the Trust in an 
application to sequestrate the Trust by a financial institution. The 
application for sequestration was also based on a suretyship of the Trust 
towards the financial institution. 
The sequestration application was opposed by the Trust represented by: 
Shepstone & Wylie, the Applicant herein. The same argument was raised 
on behalf of the Trust against Firstrand Bank which the Trust raised in the 
court a quo and the Supreme Court of Appeal (“SCA”) against the Applicant,
Page |7 
with success, if regard is had to the judgment of Poyo Diwati J (as she then 
was);® 
22.7 The Trust in the heading of the Trust deed is identified as a Property Trust; 
22.8 Notice of a date for a meeting of trustees was determined by Renata 
Mignon Volker (at the time one of 3 trustees) to be held at Tweedie, 
KwaZulu-Natal on 23 May 2013 at 11h00; 
22.9 As at 23 May 2013, the trustees of the Trust were me, Renata Mignon 
Volker (Mignon) and Thomas Wilhelm Volker (Thomas); 
22.10 From the notice of the meeting, it is apparent that item 3 on the agenda 
relate to the signing of a suretyship, which is the subject of the present 
application; 
22.11 Thomas is the ex-husband of Mignon; 
22.12 The purpose of the proposed suretyship was to bind the Trust for the 
payment of all amounts which Mignon was indebted to the Applicant in 
respect of legal fees incurred in her divorce action against Thomas, which 
together with the trusts’ alleged fees, amounted to approximately 
R2,589,208.00; 
22.13 On 21 May 2013, Thomas addressed an email to Mignon and me 
pertaining to the proposed meeting scheduled for 23 May 2013, annexure 
“C” hereto; 
22.14 On 22 May 2013 (one day before the proposed meeting) the venue of the 
meeting was changed to 143 Utrecht Street, Vryheid, KwaZulu-Natal and 
the date to 25 May 2013; 
Firstrand Bank Limited v Thomas Wilhelm Volker & Others (unreported Case No KZP 
4035/2013)
22.15 The letter, addressed by Thomas to me and Mignon is important. 
Annexure “C” is quoted verbatim: 
“Dear Abri and Mignon 
In principle, | do not have a problem with a trustees meeting as long as it 
will be a productive meeting. 
| will not be able to attend a trustees meeting this week. The notice is too 
short and | have many urgent meetings previously fixed with the 
liquidators etc. as | am employed by the liquidators, they determine my 
schedule and priorities as we work under very difficult circumstances. 
Should you wish to proceed with a meeting, please let me know so Ican 
give you dates well in advance.”; 
22.16 The meeting scheduled for 25 May 2013, notwithstanding the contents of 
the letter, proceeded in the absence of Thomas; 
22.17 \t was resolved by me and Mignon at the meeting on 25 May 2013, in the 
absence of Thomas, that the suretyship be signed by me and Mignon; 
22.18 There was no input of Thomas when it was resolved to sign the suretyship; 
22.19 The Trust Deed has an appendix thereto containing the heading “Power 
of Trustees”, which is annexed to the trust deed, annexure “B” hereto; 
22.20 The Trust Deed does not specifically state the manner how decisions of 
the trustees are to be taken and in particular that a majority of trustees 
present at the meeting may bind the minority, not present.

Now, when the shoe is on the other foot and pinching, spurious legal issues are 
raised which, so it is contended, requires the attention of this Court, which is 
opposed. 
PROSPECTS OF SUCCESS ON THE MERITS 
24.1 To determine whether there are prospects of success on appeal, it is 
necessary to first consider whether the trustees were empowered in terms 
of the Trust Deed to conclude the suretyship and second whether a valid 
resolution was passed in respect thereof; 
24.2 The provisions of the Trust Deed were considered by the SCA and had 
been correctly interpreted having regard to principles enunciated in well- 
known authority; 
24.3 The judgments of the SCA are well reasoned and are beyond criticism; 
24.4 It was correctly held by the SCA that Thomas Volker (the third trustee) was 
not present at the meeting, that the purported “resolution” was not with his 
consent, nor did he express his views on the items on the agenda. He 
simply sought a more convenient date for the trustees meeting;® 
24.5 The rules regarding the interpretation of a contract apply to the 
interpretation of the trust deed, to determine whether two trustees had the 
power to bind the Trust. The SCA had due regard thereto; 
24.6 It is respectfully submitted that the facts of each case and the terms of the 
Trust instrument will determine whether or not a decision was validly taken 
which binds the Trust;’ 
24.7 It is a trite principle that all trustees must act jointly in their dealings with 
outside persons if they are to bind the Trust;® 
Para 24 of the SCA judgment; Annexure °C”

24.8 Clauses 13.1 to 13.4 of the trust deed deals with the meetings of trustees; 
24.9 The trust deed is silent on the procedure to be followed at a meeting and 
whether a decision by the majority of the quorum present can bind the trust 
estate or whether all trustees had to be present to resolve jointly to bind the 
Trust. 
The amendment to the trust deed recorded that two trustees at a meeting 
shall constitute a quorum. This does not empower them to validly pass 
resolutions; 
24.10 There are various provisions in the trust deed that provide that the trustees 
must unanimously agree, for a decision to be valid. The following clauses 
are relevant: 
24.10.1 Clause 13.4 provides that a resolution signed by all trustees, not 
at a meeting, shall be valid. There is with respect no difference 
between a so called “Round Robin” resolution and a resolution 
taken at a meeting. 
This is indicative that all three trustees must act jointly. This was 
accepted by the SCA;° 
24.10.2 Clause 13.1 requires notice of a meeting of trustees to be given 
to all trustees who shall be given reasonable notice. Mr Volker 
clearly recorded that the notice was not reasonable and that he 
required another date. 
The SCA found that the mere alleged notice of the meeting did 
not infer participation by Thomas in the meeting;

Clause 16.1 provides that at a meeting of trustees Thomas Volker 
(the absent trustee) shall be the chairman; 
Clause 16.2 provides that in the event of disagreement arising at 
any time between trustees the majority shall prevail. This clause 
does not apply to the majority of trustees present at a meeting, 
which was confirmed by the SCA." 
The reason why three trustees (all the trustees) should be present 
is to ensure that there is no deadiock. If only two trustees are 
present and they disagree there can be no majority. 
The further reason is that in the event of an equality of votes the 
chairman, Thomas Volker, (the absent trustee) shall have a 
casting vote. This is with respect indicative that his presence was 
necessary; 
Clause 26 of the appendix expressly provides “the trustees 
unanimously agree, to conduct business on behalf of and for the 
benefit of the Trust, and to employ Trust Property in such 
business”. This further provides that all trustees must agree 
unanimously; 
This was correctly supported by Justices Kathree- Setiloane AJA 
and Weiner JA concurring; ‘2 
Clause 11.1 deals with the powers of the trustees to deal with the 
trust property. This is widely couched and refers in particular to 
the powers referred to in the appendix;

The Applicant, in the SCA relied on Firstrand Bank v Volker, Van Der 
Merwe v Hydraberg Hydraulics CC'* and Le Grange v Le Grange’® to 
submit that the deed of suretyship was validly authorised by two trustees 
present at the meeting; 
It is respectfully submitted that the SCA correctly held that the aforesaid 
cases are distinguishable on the facts.'* In the Le Grange and Van Der 
Merwe cases supra the trust deeds provide explicitly for the passing of a 
resolution in the absence of a trustee. The current trust deed in this regard 
clause 16.2 of the trust deed only provides explicitly that for “disagreements 
arising between the trustees at any time, the view of the majority shall 
prevail”. There is no express provision that the trustees present at a 
meeting (which could include only two) could bind the absent trustee. This 
clause provides for “trustees at any time” referring to the trustees appointed, 
(plural) including all trustees, which would have included Thomas Volker; 
The facts of the Le Grange case are distinguishable in that the trust deed 
contains a specific clause to the effect that the resolutions could be 
supported by a majority vote. The SCA correctly found that Le Grange case 
is distinguishable from the facts of this case as Thomas Volker did not 
abstain from voting; 
in the Van Der Merwe case the trust deed provided that decisions by 
majority vote may be taken at a quorate meeting and that such decision 
shall prevail. 
This is diametrically opposed to the provisions of the present trust deed, 
where no such provision appears;"” 
In the judgment of the 2013 litigation of Firstrand Bank Ltd, Poyo Diwati 
AJ (as she then was) correctly held in respect of the present trust deed that

The SCA correctly held that Thomas Volker did not participate in the

resolution;'9 
24.16 It is respectfully submitted that the SCA correctly relied on various 
authorities that held that unless the trust deed provides otherwise, trustees 
must act jointly.° 
It is common cause that Mr Volker did not act in any regard in executing the 
resolution to sign the deed of suretyship and the trustees therefore did not

24.17 Clause 26 of the appendix provides that all trustees must “unanimously 
agree”. This power has a direct bearing on the suretyship as correctly held

personal indebtedness of Mignon Volker’s legal fees in favour of the 
Applicant. This amount claimed is substantial and of substance. 
It is apparent from the wording of the undertaking to pay fees that the Trust 
purportedly bound itself as surety and co-principal debtor with Mignon for the 
payment of “any indebtedness or obligation of the debtor (Mignon) to the 
creditors (Shepstone & Wylie) arising from any cause whatsoever including but 
not limited to any legal costs or disbursements...;” 
It is common cause that the Trust is a property Trust and must be utilised for 
acquiring assets and property and matters incidental thereto; 
The preamble to the appendix endorses the fact that the powers must be 
exercised for the purpose and benefit of the Trust as echoed in clause 11.1; 
It is contended that the alleged suretyship had nothing to do with the purpose of 
the Trust, neither can it benefit the Trust. This was correctly held by the SCA to 
be so23. The suretyship intends to secure payment of an extraneous debt owed 
by Mignon Volker, an erstwhile trustee and beneficiary in respect of personal 
legal fees. The surety was therefore concluded for the benefit of Mignon 
personally and not for her “welfare”. It was correctly held that the power to bind 
the trust to such personal debt, fell outside the ambit of the powers of the 
trustees; 
The basic principle has been succinctly stated by Honoré: The South African 
Law of Trusts, pertaining to the powers of the Trustees to bind the Trust, at page 
197 as follows:

“In our view everything depends on the facts of each particular case and the 
provisions of the Trust instrument in question, which will determine whether or 
not a valid decision that binds a Trust has been taken;” 
30. Honoré at page 380 succinctly records the principle that Trustees must act jointly 
in their dealings with outside persons, if they are to bind the Trust estate by their 
acts by reference to Land and Agricultural Bank of South Africa v Parker. 
31. | will deal hereinafter with the aforesaid judgments which prove that the SCA 
decisions and other decisions are not in conflict with the judgment of the SCA, 
as contended by the Applicant. 
The reason for this is that there is an attack on the judgments of the SCA. 
31.1. First Rand Bank v Penvaan Property Trust (Poyo Diwati AJ, as she 
then was). 
This matter dealt with the validity of a power of attorney signed by me 
and Mignon to appoint Shepstone & Wylie to oppose an application 
instituted by the bank for the sequestration of the Trust, based on 
suretyships signed by Thomas Volker only. 
The learned Judge stated the following to Counsel instructed by 
Shepstone & Wylie: 
“| therefore agree with Mr Stokes that at all times when the suretyships 
were signed, there was always a third trustee and they did not participate 
fo such decisions and this would be contrary to the requirement that 
trustees must act jointly. It follows therefore the deeds of suretyships 
were not properly executed and the First Respondent had no authority 
to bind the Trust. The suretyships therefore are void ab initio.” 
2A 2005 (2) SA 77 SCA g

It is contended that Poyo Diwati AJ (as she then was), upon a proper 
reading of the judgment, held that in the absence of the third Trustee at 
the meeting no resolution could validly be taken on the basis that the 
trustees had to act jointly; 
31.2 The facts of the Le Grange v Le Grange®* relied on by the Applicant are 
distinguishable from the facts of this matter. 
The trust deed contained a specific clause to the effect that resolutions 
could be supported by a majority vote. 
The Penvaan Property Trust does not contain a clause to this effect. 
The Penvaan Property Trust does not provide that a majority decision 
could bind the dissenting or absent trustees; 
31.3. Van Der Merwe v Hydraberg Hydraulics. 7° 
The trust deed provided that decisions by a majority vote may be taken 
at a quorate meeting and that such decisions shall prevail. This is 
diametrically opposed to the provisions of the Penvaan Property Trust 
where no such provision appears. 
The learned Judge at 562 — 563 however stated: 
“The terms of the Trust instrument which provided for Trustees to make 
decisions by a majority vote at a quorate meeting do not provide an 
exception to the rule that all Trustees must act jointly, they merely 
provide that subject to the indemnity in clause 5.7 a majority will bind the 
dissenting or absent Trustees. The minority is obliged to act jointly with 
the other Trustees in executing the resolution, adopted by the majority,”

31.4 Honoré at page 382 states with reference to the Parker and Van Der 
Merwe cases the following: 
“As the facts of the Parker and Van Der Merwe cases illustrate, a Trust 
will consequently not be bound if (for example) two trustees of a three- 
trustee complement simply ignore the third trustee in taking a decision,” 
31.5 In Land and Agricultural Bank supra at paragraph 11, Cameron JA 
stated: 
“It follows that a provision requiring that a specified number of Trustees 
must hold office is a capacity defining condition. It lays down, a 
prerequisite that must be fulfilled before the estate can be bound”. 
At paragraph 17, Cameron JA further stated: 
“The bank contended that since the Parkers were a majority of the 
Trustees in office and since they could form a quorum at Trust meetings, 
they could bind the Trust acting together. But this is to confuse power to 
act with its due exercise.” 
This statement is instructive. The argument now presented, that 
because there was a quorum and the majority trustees in office agreed 
to the resolution, is clearly rejected by Cameron JA. 
The appendix to the trust deed which provides for the powers of the 
trustees unequivocally provides that the trustees must unanimously 
agree to conduct business on behalf of and for the benefit of the trust 
and to employ trust property in such business;

In Thorpe & Others v Tritterwein & Another’’, Scott JA stated that it 
is trite: 
“that unless the trust deed provides otherwise, trustees must act jointly.” 
This view has also been expressed in the Parkers case supra’®, 
Niewoudt & Another v Vrystaat Mielies (Edms) Bpk”? and Coetzee v 
Peet Smith Trust;*° 
Cameron JA in Land & Agricultural Bank supra at paragraph 15 stated 
the fundamental principle as follows: 
“It is a fundamental rule of Trust law which this Court recently restated in 
Niewoudt & Another v Vrystaat Mielies (Edms) Bpk that in the 
absence of contrary provision in the trust deed, the trustees must act 
jointly, if the Trust estate is to be bound by their acts.” 
It is respectfully submitted that in general, the conduct of the trust affairs 
relates to the internal business such as documents, meetings and 
decisions and external business with outsiders when the Trust estate 
could be bound; 
In Steyn & Others v Blockpave *' Rampai J explained this succinctly, 
as follows at paragraphs 37, 38 and 39: 
“| wish to add, and | do this at the risk of repeating myself that a Trust 
operates in two different spheres. Internally trustees may disagree. A 
matter on the agenda may be debated. If the trustees are not unanimous 
a matter must be put to a vote. The majority vote then prevails as the

or share for and on behalf of the Trust and execute a form of Declaration 
of Trust to the reasonable satisfaction of the Trustees. All votes 
attaching to any security, interest or share held by the Trust shall be 
exercised on behalf of the Trust by such persons (including any Trustee) and 
in such manner as the Trustees shall from time to time determine and to 
this end the Trustees shall be entitled to execute and grant such powers of 
attorney, proxies or other documents as they deem necessary. 
DISAGREEMENT BETWEEN TRUSTEES 
16.1. At and for each meeting of Trustees, the Trustees present, in person 
or by proxy, shall elect a Chairperson; provided for as long as THOMAS 
WILHELM VOLKER is a Trustee, he shall be Chairperson. 
16.2. In the event of any disagreements arising between the Trustees at any time the view of the majority shall prevail. Should there be an equality of 
votes, the Chairperson shall have a second or casting vote. 
TRUST ACCOUNTS 
17. As at the last day of February (or as at such other date as the 
Trustees shall from time to time determine) in each year, the Trustees shall cause to be framed an account of the administration of the Trust 
disclosing the Trust property, all receipts and payments made by or to the 
Trust and the manner in which the Trust property is held and invested. The 
Trustees shall have the right (but shall not be obliged) from time to time 
to appoint a practising Chartered Accountant (S.A.) to act as the auditor of the Trust and to discharge the functions referred to in this clause. 
Such auditor shall report on the accounts framed under this clause in the customary manner. 
REMUNERATION 
18. The Trustees shall from time to time determine a reasonable 
remuneration which shall be paid to them for the administration of the 
Trust. 
PROFESSIONAL FEES AND BROKERAGE 
19, Any Trustee engaged in any profession or business shall be entitled to 
charge for the services rendered to the Trust at a rate to which he or his 
firm would have been entitled in the ordinary course of his profession or 
business. 
REIMBURSEMENT OF EXPENSES 
20. All bona fide costs and expenses incurred by the Trustees in the 
administration of the Trust or the exercise of powers conferred upon them 
shall be paid by the Trustees out of the Trust income and/or Trust property as decided by the Trustees.

DELEGATION OF POWERS 
21. The Trustees shall at all times be empowered to employ an attorney, 
accountant or other agent to transact all or any business required or permitted to be done in pursuance of this Deed and to effect payment out of 
the Trust property or the income of the Trust of all charges and expenses so incurred. 
LIMITATION OF TRUSTEES LIABILITY 
22. Each Trustee is absolved from all resonsibility in the event of the 
bona fide exercise of his powers resulting in any loss of Trust property from time to time under administration or of any Trust income. No Trustees 
shall be answerable for any act, omission or negligence of any other 
Trustee or of amy attorney, accountant, or other agent employed by the 
Trustees or for any bona fide mistake in payment to any person. A Trustee 
shall not be bound to take any proceedings against a co-Trustee for any 
= preach or alleged breach of trust comitted by such co-Trustee. The Trustees shall be indemnified out of and by the Trust against all claims and demands 
of whatever nature that may be made upon them arising out of the bona fide 
exercise of any of the powers conferred under this Deed. 
DISTRIBUTION OF INCOME AND TRUST PROPERTY 
23.1 The Trustees shall use pay or apply the whole or portion of the net 
income of the Trust, in such proportions and at such time or times as they 
in their sole dicretion determine, for the welfare of all or any one or more of the beneficiaries; 
23.2 As used in 23.1 above and 24 below, "welfare" shall, besides the 
ordinary meaning of the word,also mean the benefit, comfort, maintenance, 
education, advancement and pleasure of the beneficiaries and shall include in general all those matters and purposes which the Trustees in their discretion may consider to be in the interests or for the advantage of the 
beneficiaries. 
24. The Trustees shall pay, use or apply the whole or portion of the Trust 
property in such proportions and at such time or times as they in their 
sole discretion determine, for the welfare of all or any one or more of the beneficiaries. 
25. The Trustees shall terminate the Trust after all the Trust income and Trust property will have been used, paid or applied. Should, per chance, 
all the beneficiaries be deceased and there be Trust income or Trust 
property on hand the Trustees shall, in order to terminate the Trust, pay 
or deliver such income or property to the estate or estates of one, some or 
all of the beneficiaries in such proportions as the Trustees in their discretion may determine. 
METHODS OF PAYMENT AND DELIVERY OF TRUST PROPERTY AND INCOME 
26. Any benefits payable to a beneficiary, whether before or after the 
vesting date, may be wholly or partly paid to such beneficiary personally, 
applied for the benefit of such beneficiary or invested on behalf of such 
beneficiary in any one or more investments as the Trustees consider 
appropriate. Any such payment or investment may be effected wholly or 
partly in cash or by the delivery of assets, whether of the Trust or 
otherwise. 
BENEFITS OF TRUST EXCLUSIVE TO BENEFICIARY 
27. Any benefit to which any beneficiary shall become entitled { and any 
assets acquired by virtue thereof and the income and fruits of such @enefit

PROHIBITION AGAINST ENCUMBRANCE OR DISPOSAL OF BENEFITS 
28. Unless the Trustees otherwise agree in writing, no beneficiary shall he 
entitled to cede, pledge, sell, charge or otherwise dispose of or encumber any benefit or interest to which such beneficiary is or may be entitled 
prior to its accrual to or vesting in such beneficiary.Nor shall any such 
benefit or interest be capable of attachment by any creditor of such 
beneficiary or vest in the Trustee/s of such beneficiary or his insolvent 
estate. If a beneficiary purports to cede, pledge, sell, charge or 
otherwise dispose of or encumber any such benefit or interest without the Trustee's agreement or in the event of attachment or attempted attachment 
of the whole or part of such benefit and interest or in the event of the 
insolvency of the beneficiary, the rights or expectations of such 
beneficiary in or to such benefit or interest shall cease and determine as 
though such beneficiary were dead. Notwithstanding the above, the Trustees 
may in their discretion pay, use or apply the benefit or interest such 
beneficiary would otherwise have been entitled to for the welfare of his spouse or dependants or any one or more of them. 
DIVISION INTO SUB-TRUSTS 
29. The Trustees shall have power in their absolute discretion both 
generally and specifically in the exercise of their powers under 23,24,25 and 28 above to create a sub-Trust or sub-Trusts for the benefit of any 
beneficiary or group of beneficiaries with the same Trustees as this Trust 
may then have and the Trust Deed for which shall contain substantially the same terms and conditions as this Deed does and to which the Trustees may allocate the whole or such portion of the Trust property and Trust income 
as they in their absolute discretion shall think fit. 
TRUSTEE'S INTEREST IN CONTRACTS 
30. No Trustee shall be disqualified by his office from contracting with 
the Trust or any company or firm in which the Trust is interested nor shall 
any contract entered into by the Trust or any such company or firm he invalidated or voided by reason of such interest nor shall any Trustee so 
contracting or being so interested or aquiring any benefit under any 
contract entered into with the Trust or any such company or firm be liable 
to account to the Trust for any profits or benefits realised by or under 
such contract by reason only of his holding that office;provided that he 
shall have disclosed to the other Trustees the nature of his interest 
before the making of the contract if it shall not already have been known 
to them.

33.1 no distribution shall be made by the Trustees to any person who is

interest may or could be: {i) classified or otherwise treated under such laws as a non- resident of the Republic, or
